questions.question: You are at the grocery store and suddenly feel your heart pounding, dizziness, and chest tightness during a panic attack. Which skill is the best first response?
questions.explanation: When you are in a full-blown physiological crisis like a panic attack, you need the TIPP skill because it rapidly changes your body chemistry. The 'T' for Temperature is especially powerful -- even splashing cold water on your face can activate the dive response and slow your heart rate.

questions.question: You are lying in bed trying to sleep, but your mind is stuck replaying an awkward conversation. Which skill is the best match?
questions.explanation: Your mind is stuck in a thought spiral, replaying the past. The 5-4-3-2-1 Grounding skill is perfect for pulling your attention out of your thoughts and into the present moment by focusing on your senses -- the weight of your blanket, the hum of the air conditioning, the faint glow of a streetlight.

questions.question: You received disappointing news and feel a deep wave of sadness and frustration, but you are not in a full crisis. Which skill is best?
questions.explanation: This is a powerful but not-quite-crisis emotion. Riding the Wave allows you to acknowledge and sit with the sadness without judgment, letting it pass naturally instead of fighting it and making it stronger.

questions.question: What is the recommended decision framework for choosing the right skill?

options.text: Always start with Riding the Wave, then try other skills if that fails
Check your body first (crisis = TIPP), then your mind (thought spiral = Grounding), then your emotions (strong but manageable = Riding the Wave)
Use whichever skill you practiced most recently
Start with Grounding in every situation, then escalate to TIPP if needed

questions.explanation: The framework is: (1) Check your body -- if you are in a physiological crisis, use TIPP. (2) Check your mind -- if racing thoughts or rumination are the problem, use Grounding. (3) Check your emotions -- if the emotion is strong but manageable, Ride the Wave.

questions.question: Can you combine multiple DBT skills in a single difficult situation?

options.text: No, using more than one skill at a time is counterproductive
Yes, you can use TIPP to get out of the red zone, then Grounding to anchor yourself, then Ride the Wave as the emotion fades
Only if a therapist recommends combining them
Yes, but only Grounding and Riding the Wave can be combined

questions.explanation: Skills can absolutely be combined in sequence. You might use TIPP to get out of the crisis zone, then Grounding to anchor yourself in the present, and then Ride the Wave as the remaining emotion naturally fades. Any skill used is better than none.

title: DBT Skills Challenge: Choosing the Right Tool - Quiz
